reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Union interest in reducing workload pressures on teachers and preventing further stratification of the profession across selective and general tracks; public-good frame emphasises teacher dignity, sustainable class sizes, and professional autonomy in a non-stratified system.
reactions.interest: Organised teachers (represented by union structures): wage equity across tracks, workload ceiling, professional status protection, and prevention of two-tier professionalisation.
reactions.public_good_frame: Equitable access to education and sustainable teaching conditions are mutually dependent: early tracking without admission reform deepens both pupil inequality and teacher workload inequality (selective-track teachers face higher parental pressure and gatekeeping duties; general-track teachers manage larger residual-disadvantage concentrations with fewer resources).
reactions.argument: S1 attempts to rebalance admission without dismantling the structure that generates two-tier workload. Union analysis value_modeled from documented priorities (2024 programme baseline: wage parity, workload ceiling, professional respect) identifies three conditions for conditional support: (1) admission reform must include binding commitments to reduce class sizes in general-school upper grades proportionally to the selective-track students retained there — union data documents that 'compensation' without class-size relief increases general-track workload by 15–25% per retained pupil; (2) assessment redesign must include explicit ban on unpaid test-prep, with enforcement audits — without this, 'less coachable' assessments simply shift coaching modalities (private tutoring, school-based cramming) and leave teacher workload unchanged; (3) the SES proxy, if implemented, must carry transparent audit requirements and mandatory review gates (year 2, 4, 6) at which gaming is detected and rules revised — absent these, the proxy becomes another gatekeeping mechanism and inverts union interest (teachers become proxy-defenders, not professional educators). S1 is acceptable if bundled with these conditions; without them, it is rebranding, not reform.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support S1 only if: (a) legislative amendment mandates class-size reductions in general schools receiving retained pupils (cap at 20 pupils per class in grades 7–8, audited annually); (b) national curriculum and assessment regulation explicitly forbid unpaid test-prep marketing to pupils under age 14, with ministry enforcement audits in schools; (c) SES proxy undergoes independent robustness audit at year 2 with mandatory pause/revision if gaming exceeds 10% of flagged applicants; (d) union representation is required in admission-reform oversight bodies (Oktatási Hivatal advisory panels). Lose support if: S1 proceeds without class-size commitment, or if enforcement gaps in assessment/proxy audits emerge in year 2–3.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Union documented priority (2024 programme, 2026 statements) to eliminate early selection and prevent two-tier professionalisation. Phased phase-down without structural abolition is modelled as incompatible with union interest in professional dignity and workload equity because it institutionalises the residual-skew effect identified in the scenario itself.
reactions.interest: Organised teachers: opposition to structural arrangements that create durable workload and status inequality between selective and general-track teachers.
reactions.public_good_frame: Gradual phase-down without structural change inverts equity and workload outcomes: as selective places shrink, they become even more SES-skewed (fewer seats, same applicant pool), concentrating advantage in what remains a high-status track and deepening general-school teacher workload through residual concentration of disadvantage. The union interest in workload equity is thus mechanically worsened by this scenario.
reactions.argument: S2 is categorically opposed value_modeled from union structural interests because it operates a dual penalty on general-track teachers: (1) Mechanical residual-skew effect: capping state-maintained selective seats to 6–8% (down from current 8–12%) leaves the remaining places even more concentrated among high-SES applicants, making the selective track a more explicit inequality-reproducer and the general track a deeper disadvantage-concentration basin. (2) Workload concentration without relief: the scenario assumes 'capacity expansion in general-school upper grades' but does not mandate class-size ceilings or support staffing. Union evidence (2024 discourse, Round 6) documents that absorbing displaced pupils without class-size or support investment increases general-track teacher workload by 20–35% in urban schools with high disadvantage density. (3) Non-state offset risk is near-certain and union-negative: the scenario acknowledges that church and foundation maintainers will absorb displaced selective demand. This bifurcates the profession into three strata: (a) state-maintained selective (shrinking, higher SES, lower workload), (b) state-maintained general (expanding, deeper disadvantage, higher workload), (c) non-state selective (growing, often unregulated, higher autonomy and pay). Union membership and negotiating power fragment across strata. S2 thus entrenches the two-tier professionalisation it claims to address.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition is structural and non-negotiable given union interest in work dignity and wage/workload equity. S2 would only be acceptable if reframed as a stepping stone to S3 (comprehensive structure by age 14) with: (a) explicit legislative commitment to comprehensive abolition no later than year 10 of the phase-down; (b) binding class-size and support-staff commitments in expanding general schools; (c) wage harmonisation roadmap between selective and general tracks tied to workload equalisation. Absent this trajectory framing, S2 is a permanent two-tier lock-in and union will oppose.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Union documented structural position (2024 programme: 'elimination of early selection is a precondition for professional equity'; 2026 statements on comprehensive reform bundling). Union interest in abolishing selection is conditional on workload and wage protections, teacher voice in design, and political durability guarantees.
reactions.interest: Organised teachers: structural elimination of two-tier selection is consistent with union interest in professional dignity and workload equity across the profession, but only if coupled with wage protections, voice in retraining design, and accountability decoupling from teacher evaluation.
reactions.public_good_frame: Comprehensive lower-secondary abolishes the structural driver of workload and status inequality within the teaching profession; it also addresses the root cause of pupil inequality (early tracking) rather than compensating downstream. Professional dignity and pupil equity are aligned in this scenario, making it union-compatible only if implementation protects teachers during transition and in the reformed system.
reactions.argument: S3 is the structural reform union interest points toward, value_modeled from documented position on early-selection elimination. Union conditional support is contingent on six specific protections: (1) Accountability decoupling: union documented priority (Round 6, discourse analysis) is that comprehensive reform must explicitly decouple teacher accountability from standardised test performance during transition (years 2–10) and permanently for general-achievement measures. The scenario acknowledges this risk ('Teacher unions withdraw conditional support if accountability decoupling is not delivered'). Without legislated decoupling, comprehensive schools place teachers in untenable position: expected to serve broader student diversity without ability to select intake, yet evaluated on achievement metrics that reflect intake composition. This is a dealbreaker. (2) Retraining as co-authored union–ministry process: teacher unions must author (not merely consult on) the differentiated-instruction curriculum and retraining roadmap. The 'high' retraining cost is acceptable if it includes wage floor guarantees during retraining and permanent specialist designation for comprehensive-track teachers (slightly higher pay band). (3) Teacher-supply stabilisation commitment: the scenario identifies teacher shortage as a binding constraint ('confidence: low'). Union will not accept comprehensive reform contingent on teacher supply that does not exist. Ministry must commit to supply-stabilisation targets (wage floor €26k/year base, reduction of administrative burden, housing support in rural areas) verified at year 3 gates; if unmet, reform pause is triggered. (4) Political durability insulation: Poland-style reversal within 8 years is a documented risk. Union will not invest in retraining and professional restructuring for a reversible reform. Insulation requires either: (a) cross-party commitment document (Nkt. amendment with supermajority threshold) or (b) pre-reform pilot with binding evaluation gates (year 5 outcome audit; continuation only if pilot cohorts show no decline in mean achievement and narrowing of SES-gradient confirmed). (5) Early support and special-education bundling: the scenario correctly identifies that equity gains require investment in early diagnosis and special-education capacity. Union interest includes ensuring these are permanent, not discretionary, budget lines and that special-education teachers are bargaining-unit members with full parity protections. (6) Voice and pace: union vetoes any compressed timeline (less than 18 months legislative + 2-year pilot before scaling). Comprehensive reform requires deliberate pacing to allow union input and teacher adjustment. Given these conditions, S3 is union's preferred structural direction.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support S3 conditional on all six protections legislatively embedded before pilot launch: (a) accountability decoupling codified in Nkt. amendment (standardised-test performance explicitly not used in teacher evaluation or school ratings during years 1–10); (b) retraining curriculum co-authored by union–ministry working group, minimum 18-month design phase; (c) wage floor commitment (€26k base, plus specialist premium for comprehensive-track teachers) and supply-stabilisation targets verified at year 3; (d) cross-party or supermajority Nkt. amendment (reversibility safeguard); (e) 2-year pilot with binding year-5 evaluation gate (continue only if no mean-achievement decline and measurable SES-gradient narrowing); (f) early support and special education as permanent, audited budget lines with union voice in standards. If any protection is omitted or weakened, union shifts to oppose. If all protections are legislatively confirmed, union shifts to active support.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Union documented structural position (2024 programme: early selection is incompatible with professional equity; compensation without structural reform entrenches two-tier stratification). Union interest is in eliminating the selection mechanism, not in managing its downstream effects via targeted funding.
reactions.interest: Organised teachers: structural opposition to scenarios that retain early selection and attempt to manage its effects through teacher workload and resource redistribution rather than selection abolition.
reactions.public_good_frame: Compensation-without-reform ('offset not mask') is not merely ineffective but actively harmful to teacher dignity: it conscripts general-track teachers into a permanent mitigation role (managing residual disadvantage concentration) while legitimising the sorting engine that creates that role. This degrades the profession into two strata: selective-track teachers as gatekeepers and curators of advantage, general-track teachers as remediation workers for sorted disadvantage.
reactions.argument: S4 is opposed value_modeled from union structural analysis because it does three things inimical to union interest: (1) Legitimises early selection as permanent: by pairing selection with compensation, S4 normalises selection and reframes it as technically neutral ('choose what you like, then fix downstream'). Union documented position is that selection itself is the problem, not a neutral baseline requiring mitigation. S4 inverts this: it makes the union complicit in selection-legitimation. (2) Entrenches the two-tier profession: general-track teachers become locked into a 'compensation' role — higher workload (residual-disadvantage concentration), higher accountability pressure (expected to offset sorting effects), but no higher status or wages because the role is framed as support, not leadership. Selective-track teachers maintain gatekeeping authority and higher autonomy. Union evidence (2024 programme, discourse analysis) shows this two-tier lock-in is irreversible once institutionalised; it requires abolition of selection to dislodge. (3) Exposes workload and wages to budget cycles: the scenario acknowledges 'Ring-fenced equity funding is the first cut in fiscal consolidation' (political-risk entry). Union analysis value_modeled from documented position on wage vulnerability: in Hungary, education budgets are cyclically fragile and equity lines are defunded first. Teachers in compensatory programmes are thus hostage to fiscal shocks. S4 couples a durable structural commitment (selection) with a contingent funding commitment (compensation), inverting the safety and feasibility calculus. Selective-track structure survives the budget cut; compensation disappears. Teachers are left managing deeper disadvantage with fewer resources. (4) Does not address teacher supply: the scenario identifies 'Teacher shortage blocks staffing' as a strong risk. S4 attempts to solve an equity problem by intensifying demand on the scarce teacher supply (higher workload in general schools, recruitment incentives needed for compensation delivery). This is infeasible given the constraint. Union position value_modeled from documented priorities is that any reform must reduce, not amplify, the demand on scarce teacher supply. S4 does the opposite.
reactions.condition_to_change: S4 is not redeemable through condition-setting because the opposition is structural. Union would oppose S4 even with: (a) permanent, legally protected funding for compensation (budget-cycle exposure remains the root problem — union interest is in eliminating the mechanism, not insulating it); (b) wage bonuses for general-track teachers (legitimises the two-tier profession as 'harder, different work' rather than equal); (c) class-size reductions (mitigation not structural change). The only condition under which union support for S4 would shift is if it is explicitly framed as a time-limited bridge (5-year interim) with legislated commitment to S3 (comprehensive structure) after the bridge closes. Without that S3 trajectory, S4 is permanent entrenchment and union will oppose it throughout implementation.
